title: Common Entrance English: Comprehension, Grammar, and Essay Writing
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The English paper in the National Common Entrance Examination tests three core skills: reading comprehension, grammatical accuracy, and written expression. Many students who speak English fluently still lose marks because they have not practiced the specific skills the exam requires. Speaking English and writing it under exam conditions are different abilities. Here is what your child needs to master for each section of the English paper.

The Comprehension Section

The comprehension section presents a passage followed by questions. The passage may be a story, a factual description, or a short article. The questions test whether your child understood what they read. Some questions ask for specific information that can be lifted directly from the passage. Others ask your child to infer meaning, identify the main idea, or explain the meaning of a word or phrase as used in context.

Teach your child to read the questions before reading the passage. This gives their reading a purpose. They know what information to look for. When they find a sentence that answers a question, they should underline it. This saves time when writing answers and reduces the chance of missing key details. The technique is simple, but many students never learn it.

Answers should be based on the passage, not on what your child already knows about the topic. If the passage says something that contradicts what your child learned in school, the passage is the authority for the purpose of the exam. A student who answers from their own knowledge rather than from the passage will lose marks, even if their answer is factually correct.

Encourage your child to write answers in complete sentences unless the question specifically asks for a single word or phrase. A complete sentence demonstrates understanding more clearly than a fragment. It also reduces the chance that the examiner will misinterpret an incomplete answer.

The Grammar Section

The grammar section tests your child's knowledge of the rules of English. Common topics include parts of speech, such as nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs, tenses, including past, present, and future, subject-verb agreement, punctuation, including capital letters, full stops, commas, and question marks, and direct and indirect speech.

These topics are rule-based. Once your child understands the rule, they can apply it to any question. The most effective way to prepare is to review each topic systematically. Explain the rule. Show examples. Then practice with questions. The practice should include both correct applications of the rule and common errors that the exam might test.

Pay special attention to tenses. Many students struggle with irregular verbs and with the distinction between the simple past and the present perfect. Questions that ask your child to fill in the correct form of a verb are common and often answered incorrectly. Practice these specifically.

Punctuation is another area where marks are frequently lost. Students often forget to use capital letters at the beginning of sentences or for proper nouns. They may use commas incorrectly or fail to use question marks for questions. These errors are easy to fix once your child is aware of them. Review the basic rules and practice applying them in writing.

The Essay Section

The essay section asks your child to write a composition on a given topic. The topic may be narrative, asking them to tell a story, descriptive, asking them to describe a person, place, or event, or expository, asking them to explain something. Your child will have a choice of topics, usually three or four. Choosing the right topic is the first important decision.

Teach your child to choose the topic they can write about most comfortably, not the one that sounds most interesting. A simple topic written about well scores higher than an ambitious topic written about poorly. Before writing, your child should spend a few minutes planning. What are the main points? What order will they appear in? A planned essay is organized and clear. An unplanned essay often wanders and loses the reader.

The essay should have a clear beginning, middle, and end. The beginning introduces the topic. The middle develops it with details, examples, or events. The end concludes the essay, summarizing or reflecting on what has been written. This structure is simple, but it works. An essay with a clear structure and average content will score higher than a brilliant but disorganized essay.

Encourage your child to write in paragraphs. Each paragraph should develop one main idea. A new idea means a new paragraph. The paragraphs should be clearly separated. This makes the essay easier to read and signals to the examiner that your child understands how to organize their writing.

Grammar and spelling matter in the essay section. A well-structured essay with poor grammar will lose marks. Encourage your child to leave a few minutes at the end to read through their work and correct any obvious errors. The review catches mistakes that are invisible during the rush of writing.

How to Practice at Home

Reading is the foundation of English skills. Encourage your child to read regularly, both fiction and non-fiction. The reading builds vocabulary, improves comprehension, and exposes your child to correct grammar and sentence structure. A child who reads widely will find the English paper easier than a child who does not.

Practice writing essays under timed conditions. Give your child a topic and thirty minutes to write. The timed practice builds the skill of organizing thoughts quickly and writing under pressure. After the time is up, review the essay together. Point out strengths before weaknesses. Celebrate what was done well before discussing what could be improved.

Use past question papers for grammar and comprehension practice. The more familiar your child is with the exam format, the more confident they will be on exam day.

The Bottom Line

The Common Entrance English paper tests comprehension, grammar, and essay writing. Teach your child to read questions before passages in the comprehension section. Review grammar topics systematically, with special attention to tenses and punctuation. For the essay, choose the most manageable topic, plan before writing, and structure the essay with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Practice at home through regular reading, timed essay writing, and past question practice. The students who perform best in English are not necessarily the most naturally gifted writers. They are the ones who understand what the exam expects and prepare accordingly.
